sentences.text: Right.
And when he came there, in the meantime my sister-in-law had moved.
So another Gentile, who I had played soccer with when I was still living in Amsterdam, he saw somebody looking and he asked the man who you're looking for.
And he knew my sister-in-law had moved.
So, and him knowing-- the Gentile knowing-- that my brother-in-law did underground work, he said to the man that I might be able to find out for you where they live.
Because he didn't want to go with the man straight to that house where my sister-in-law lives.
So mind you, he would come back to-- I was living in between the-- and he, the Gentile, helped my sister-in-law-- protected her.
She was Jewish.
He was not.
My brother-in-law was not Jewish.
And that way, my sister-in-law contacted him, the man who brought the letter to Holland for me.
And she sent you what?
You name it.
She sent me as much as she could.
She sent me even oranges, through those guys.
She even sometimes, one went and the other one came-- another one back with the food.
She sent me rice.
Now what are you going to do with rice?
Sure enough, in the camp, we had a-- we found always a brick, with a wire, and we improvised a cooking utensil, you know?
But I forgot to tell you that even the pictures they send me, I got that written down.
In one of the letters that I sent her, that I said the pictures were nice.
Her brother-- my sister-in-law-- was living in the same camp with me.
So, what she send to me, was only half for me.
Even though it was for me.
But I shared it with him.
Unbelievable-- unbelievable.
And where are those letters?
Where are those letters?
There was a man in Amsterdam, in the Netherlands-- I think Amsterdam-- he wrote 11 books about the war, about the Jewish and all.
My sister-in-law's-- my sister-in-law's husband who worked for the underground, I told you a couple of times-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] my sister-in-law was asked by-- and she kept the letters.
She kept the pieces, the [INAUDIBLE] and everything.
Later on there was a Belgian guy, Armand Felinder-- he wrote my writing on his card, so that the handwriting was his.
And he also worked in the [NON-ENGLISH], and he a case of his tools.
And when he received a package, he put it in the case and he told me-- that he couldn't hand it to me because they could have killed him if they saw me giving him, giving me something.
And I took it out of this tool case.
He told me where it was.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] My sister-in-law, then, oh-- 11 times-- so the man who wrote the books asked my sister-in-law if he could have those letters for the War Documentation in the Netherlands.
So she wrote me.
I said, go ahead.
So the War Documentation from the Netherlands have those letters.
One day, about seven, eight years ago, my daughter went to the Netherlands-- where she is right now, also-- she went to the War Documentation, and she asked if she could see it.
She saw it.
It was just in a drawer-- it was not on display.
That burned me up.
And they made copies for her.
But copies they typed the words that I had written in-- in Blechhammer.
They typed up all Dutch words on paper.
Sure enough, red tape, she had to pay 3 guilder-- a dollar and a half for it.
I donated it.
My daughter have to pay 3 guilders for it.
Really miserable, you know?
Really miserable.
But it's the red tape in the Netherlands.
When the Holocaust Museum in Washington started to build, I wrote to them and I got word from Lisa Topelmann.
And I told her, I made a copy from pictures what I might show you later.
I wrote them what I just told you about the-- and I said, I'm going to write to the Netherlands, if they want to share half with your museum.
If I had known, I'd never given it to them-- all of them.
The Netherlands wrote me back-- no.
I wrote to Lisa Topelmann again.
I wrote the Netherlands, again.
I got a stern letter from them, You donated it.
It is belong now to the Kingdom of the Netherlands.
From December 4, 1942 to January 21, 1945.
We walked for two weeks, till that second Saturday night.
Till we arrived in Gross Rosen.
And if I may say so, we start walking with 4,000-- approximately 4,500 people-- 4,500.
I don't think we arrived with 2,000.
One of them was my nephew, my aunt's son.
And I was maybe one of the youngest because I was 18, 19 years when I was in the camp in Kremboong.
I wasn't even 19, because in 1942, I was 18 years, and in the camp I turned 19.
Men-- six feet tall, I had-- I had to give them strength to walk those two weeks.
Every night, and again, they told me, Max, I'm not going to go tomorrow because I can't, I can't.
I said, we got to go.
Till the last night before we got to Gross Rosen-- my nephew gave up.
Because I'd never see him again.
What they did, we were-- we slept overnight in haystacks.
About the same age as I.
Isidore Rothfeld.
He didn't make it, no.
I traced it once on the map, but-- where he might have died, but-- I got a map from Poland, you know.
I used to-- got the date last night.
It's either the 3rd or the 4th of February.
I got it on paper.
A few days.
A week or so.
I don't know.
By train again to Buchenwald.
From Buchenwald I rested a while, also, a week, or two weeks, I don't know.
Oh, before we hit Buchenwald I got bombed in the train in Weimar.
That the English or the American came over, and they bombed the-- the-- the train stations there.
Because it was all the raw material they tried to put out of commission.
And we were in open train so we saw the bombs falling in the air.
But not the first time we saw bomb falling.
Many times we saw bomb falling.
[LAUGHS] I saw people hanged.
What could I do?
I was sitting in the train, an open train-- cattle train.
10, 10 of us.
I was in Buchenwald, after Buchenwald we went to--
In Buchenwald date-- is that just the 4th maybe? 8th, or so. 8th of February or so.
A week, 10 days, maybe, yeah.
In Buchenwald, itself?
In Buchenwald I didn't have a bed assigned to me.
Slept on the floor, under the tables, wherever I found some crumbs of bread.
No work.
No.
When there was something to eat they fed you, when there was nothing--
We left-- in my group 500, 700, didn't count.
Didn't know how to-- know now that there was--
Why did I leave?
They send me away again there.
Got me.
I don't know why they sent me away.
They had no use for me there and they wanted me to work somewhere else where there was work for me.
They're not going to feed-- they're not going to feed you if you don't work.
Where they sent me?
They sent me to-- was it-- where did I go from Buchenwald?
I have to look it up.
I owe you that.
I've got to look it up.
I got it on paper.
Where I was liberated?
Also a story, in itself.
They put us in the train.
We finally wound up in Dachau in Allach.
So they put us on the train, and then it was near Dachau.
And we were four days on the train-- it was not an open train it was closed on top.
Four days on the train before an engine came-- finally.
We had a little can.
And there was a little-- on the top there was a little air vent, or something, whatever it was was open.
Out went the can.
The urine was thrown away, and the next one to could urinate.
But we suffered.
Indescribable.
Were we fed?
No.
No.
They must have known something was coming.
Because one day, we went back and forth in the train.
One day, sure enough, every one of us got a package from the Red Cross.
Every one of us-- cigarettes-- you name it-- corned beef, everything in it.
We were eating it.
And we was eating and we were-- we had to get rid of it.
We got rid of it.
It went through because our stomach was not set for food anymore.
Especially greasy food.
So one night, we made another stop.
We waited and waited and waited.
And I was young.
I picked up a couple languages there.
Not too much, but little basics.
One day we were drinking water, and soldiers were guarding us.
I was the last one to drink water.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] A soldier said to me, [POLISH]?
That means, 'Do you speak Polish?' Because most likely I was a Polish Jew.
I told him, no.
He spoke English.
He told me that I was the last one there.
We're going to bring you to the Americans, he told me.
Back in the train, people who didn't speak Dutch, I told them in German what happened.
Everybody started to pray.
Everybody.
Sure enough, the next morning, the Americans came.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They took the Germans, with three, four tanks, with the cannon in the center-- Germans in the center.
Some of us wanted to get even with the German soldiers, because one of the German soldiers killed a brother of a Jew.
The Americans didn't let us.
Makes sense.
They saw after us, the Americans, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] fed us, and then they had a meal for us one night, that first night-- had a meal for-- [INAUDIBLE].
They asked for English, they asked for if there were French people, and they asked, and third, it was the Dutch people.
Because it's Allied.
They fed us good.
But they couldn't understand that we couldn't hold the food.
So some of us wound up in hospital, and even one of us died.
His name is, he was the head of the Jewish commission in the Netherlands.
And I, for one was told that his German doctors about his condition, and I was the first one was told by the German who was in charge there, that he died.
In Feldafing.
I looked the name up last night again.
I owe you that.
I don't know exactly.
It might be F-E-L-D-D-A-F-I-N-G I'll have to look it up.
It was no camp.
It was out in the fields.
Open fields.
A day.
And then we searched to get back to the Netherlands.
But it was not easy because first of all, I stole a bicycle [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] to search for food.
Not only for me.
Oh, yeah-- I skipped a lot.
And a whole lot I skipped.
And there were three of us who said, one for one, and all for each other.
And so I was trying to  get some food.
And one of us couldn't walk, and that bicycle-- and we went from town to town.
The one who couldn't  walk was sitting on the bike and we were holding the bike with him on top.
Now the same two guys-- I'm going to tell you something.
We walked for  two weeks-- I told you before.
You might say I jump-- but I want everything out there.
I want the world to know-- the future-- what's happened to me, and to us.
The three of us said, if there is one piece of bread, we're going to share the three.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The first one went into a house and stole a loaf of bread off the table.
Desperate.
Desperate.
Shared it with three.
My turn.
I went into a house-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] wooden shoes-- not wooden shoes from Holland, but what we got up in the camp.
No socks.
Just [NON-ENGLISH], rags, no decent clothes in January in Poland.
I went into the house.
I put-- we had a blanket over us.
Everybody had a blanket who walked.
I went into the house.
I stole a pan of potatoes off the stove.
I got to survive.
You can hold it against me.
Hold it against me, OK.
I had it under my blanket.
I had about 20 people-- Jewish people like me.
They wanted a potato.
I didn't blame them.
But if you get 20 people around, and you are guarded by soldiers, the soldiers come.
What happened?
One Hitler unit-- was watching us while we were walking there-- saw it.
Took out his gun.
Me with the potatoes in my-- a pan of potatoes under my blanket.
He was ready to shoot me.
And a German soldier didn't let him shoot me.
That was between January 21st and February 1st-- February 4th-- between Blechhammer and Gross Rosen.
Now, they say it was Poland, it was [NON-ENGLISH], it was-- I think it was that area there.
I don't know exactly.
Dachau was at the end of the war.
Was it [NON-ENGLISH], then?
Gross Rosen-- I don't know where it was.
I didn't look where am I going.
I'm travel I was sent there.
Yeah, now, it was not an area-- area within Poland, [NON-ENGLISH], wherever it was, is immaterial.
It is what they did to us.
I don't think it's material if Poland or no Poland.
I told you a minute ago, we went back and forth.
I don't know where it was.
And I don't care where it was.
I only looked after my health and after my life.
They-- the Germans-- when I was in a small town, they send us-- the German sought whoever, no, the Americans they did transportation.
They bought us to Ulm.
U-L-M.
In this place of Ulm.
From there-- they-- we were a day or two, maybe the same day-- they sent us on the Autobahn to Mannheim.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And from Mannheim, I was put in a train to the Netherlands.
I went to Eindhoven. 12 It's in the south of the Netherlands, because we couldn't go the bridge on account of there was no bridge.
It was bombed.
There was a movie made from that, and the guy who made it was a Dutch fellow by the name of-- well, it's immaterial.
Anyway, that was-- and-- they gave us sleeping arrangement in Eindhoven, in the well-known company, Phillips.
Phillips makes all electrical things-- you name it-- one of the biggest in the world.
And from there, they were sent to private homes.
And the name of the place, the small town near Eindhoven, the name of this place was Geldrop.
G-E-L-D-R-O-P.
I was-- maybe a week or so till my brother-in-law who worked for the underground, as I told you before, Gentile, came and brought me back to Amsterdam.
Through the Red Cross.
I moved in with my sister-in-law.

If I may say so, for posterity, for the future to know, that when I came back one Saturday night, we got to know that my sister-in-law's-- my in-law parents came back from wherever they were, I think Theresienstadt, or whatever.
And they had to be picked up from the train station-- Central station-- I've got a picture that had been hanging there of that station.
I had to pick them up to come back also from Germany.
And I was sitting in a taxi, I think it was.
I was sitting with the driver and they were sitting behind me.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] They asked me-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they didn't face me, because they were sitting in back of me-- they called the names.
Did this one come back?
I'd say, no.
Every one of the children, they asked me-- I had to say no.
That was something for a man, 22 years old I was at the time, to tell my in-laws that their children didn't come back.
[PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] I went through something in my life, for what Hitler started.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I didn't live long life, I lived more than one life in all different ways.
My second wife, like my first wife, I met her at a dance.
In 19-- September of 1945.
She lost her husband.
I lost my wife.
And we were-- we know each other's-- each other's pain.
And she had only a sister.
I had no blood relative, only my nephew.
And I found out later who was with a farmers.
I've got a picture of them.
They took him in as a boy-- as a baby.
He was born in '41-- November '41.
And they took him, I think, in '43, because he was a year and a half, the farmers took him in, gave him a different name.
When I saw-- when I saw him, they didn't even have to tell me it was him.
He looked like his father.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] There were three kids living there-- I knew it was him.
I found out one day he was alive, with the farmers who took him in.
The next day I was on the train-- the whole family, two people.
And then they tell me, it was a hoax.
It was a hoax.
My second wife, I met at a dance in  1945-- September 1945.
She was only with her sister, and I was all by myself.
She was a survivor- yeah.
She and her sister were both survivors.
How long did I what?
Court her?
Oh-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] from September to-- in a different kind of courting than it is now.
I mean, we both had sores, we both had pains.
We didn't have any money to our accounts.
When I came back from Poland I was penniless-- not even a penny to my account.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And I got a hold of some money-- one way or another.
I-- we start living together, even though it was with her sister, in her sister's house.
Because those two sisters were-- were married to two brothers.
And those two brothers didn't come back, but the third brother came back. my sister-in-law married her brother-in-law.
And those two sisters who were almost always together, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we moved in with-- moved in with them.
